walkthrough.title: Place the Machine Tile
walkthrough.text: Let's get Turing Machine set up. First, find the Machine tile. It's the large rectangular tile that acts as the center of your play area. Place it in the middle of the table where everyone can see it. This tile is the heart of the machine you'll be interrogating.

walkthrough.title: Assemble the Punch Card Support
walkthrough.text: Now grab the punch card support. This is the plastic stand that holds all your punch cards upright. Snap it together if this is your first game. Good news: you don't need to take it apart when you're done. Just store it assembled in the box. Stand it up near the Machine tile where everyone can reach it.

walkthrough.title: Load the Punch Cards
walkthrough.text: Find the 45 punch cards. There are three sets of 15 cards, one for each color: blue, yellow, and purple. Each set has cards numbered 1 through 5 with three copies of each number. Place them in the support with all the blue cards in the front, followed by all the yellow cards, then all the purple cards in the back. The cards have small holes punched in them, which is how you'll read the Verifiers' answers.

walkthrough.title: Choose a Problem
walkthrough.text: Now you need to pick a problem to solve. The rulebook contains 20 problems, numbered 1 through 20. Problems 1 through 4 are introductory and perfect for first-time players. Each problem tells you which Criteria cards and Verification cards to use, and how many Verifiers you'll have. For your very first game, use problem 1. You can also find millions more problems on the Turing Machine website at turingmachine.info by selecting Classic mode.

walkthrough.title: Place the Criteria Cards
walkthrough.text: The problem you chose lists specific Criteria card numbers for each Verifier. Find those Criteria cards from the deck of 48 and place each one in front of its assigned Verifier letter. For example, problem 1 uses Verifier A with card 4, Verifier B with card 9, Verifier C with card 11, and Verifier D with card 14. Each Criteria card shows what that Verifier is checking, along with a list of possible criteria at the bottom. Your job during the game is to figure out which criterion is the right one.

walkthrough.title: Place the Verification Cards
walkthrough.text: Now find the Verification cards. The problem also lists a specific Verification card number for each Verifier. Place each Verification card face down in front of its assigned Verifier, right next to the Criteria card. Important: tick the corresponding letter (A, B, C, or D) on the back of each Verification card to avoid mixing them up later. Each Verification card can be used for 4 different numbers and 4 different colors, so the alignment with your punch cards matters.

walkthrough.title: Hand Out Player Materials
walkthrough.text: Give each player a Player Aid card, which doubles as a privacy screen, and a note sheet from the pad of 50. The note sheet is where you'll track your proposals, the answers you get from Verifiers, and your deductions. If you're playing a problem from the online app, write down the game number on your sheet. If your problem uses fewer than 6 Verifiers, draw a line through the unused Verifier columns (E and F for a 4-Verifier game) so you don't accidentally try to use them.

walkthrough.title: Understand the Goal
walkthrough.text: Before you start, make sure everyone understands the goal. You're trying to find a secret 3-digit code made up of three numbers between 1 and 5. The first digit is blue, the second is yellow, and the third is purple. Each Verifier checks for one specific criterion, like 'the blue number is greater than 3' or 'the three numbers are in ascending order.' Once you deduce all the criteria from all the Verifiers, you can figure out the one code that satisfies every single one. Ready? Let's play.

walkthrough.title: Simultaneous Rounds
walkthrough.text: Turing Machine is played in rounds, and here's something unusual: everyone plays at the same time. There are no turns. Each round, all players individually and simultaneously go through the same four steps: Compose, Question, Deduce, and End of Round. You're not questioning other players like in most deduction games. Instead, you're all questioning the same machine. Let's break down each step.

walkthrough.title: Step 1: Compose Your Proposal
walkthrough.text: To start each round, compose a 3-digit proposal by pulling three punch cards from the support. Pick one blue card (your first digit), one yellow card (your second digit), and one purple card (your third digit). Each card is numbered 1 through 5, so your proposal is a 3-digit number like 2-4-3. Overlay the three cards on top of each other. In the rare case that another player is using a card you need, just wait until they're done. Important: once you compose your proposal, you must keep it the same for the entire round. You cannot change your proposal between questions.

walkthrough.title: Step 2: Question the Verifiers
walkthrough.text: Now you question up to 3 Verifiers in a single round. You can always question fewer than 3 if you want. To question a Verifier, take their Verification card and slide it under your three overlapping punch cards. Make sure the alignment symbols in the corners line up properly. Through the hole in your overlapping punch cards, you'll see the Verifier's answer: either a checkmark (your proposal passed) or an X (your proposal failed). Write the answer on your note sheet, then put the Verification card back face down in front of its Verifier immediately.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Verifier Answers
walkthrough.text: This is the trickiest part for new players, so pay close attention. When a Verifier gives you a checkmark, it means your proposal passes their specific test. When they give you an X, it means your proposal fails. But here's the key: the answer only tells you about the criterion being tested, not about the actual secret code. For example, if Verifier A checks whether 'the blue number is greater than 3,' and you propose blue 2, you'll get an X. That tells you the criterion might be 'blue is greater than 3,' because 2 is not greater than 3. But it does NOT tell you anything about what the actual blue number in the code is. The Verifier doesn't know the code. It only knows its own criterion.

walkthrough.title: Recording Your Answers
walkthrough.text: It is mandatory to write down your proposal and the answer from every Verifier you question on your note sheet. Write V for a checkmark or X for a fail in the proper column. This is not optional. Recording answers is critical for two reasons. First, your deductions depend on accurate notes. Second, the total number of questions you've asked determines the winner in case of a tie. Each V or X you write counts as one question. Keep your answers and notes secret behind your Player Aid screen, but your proposal and which Verifiers you question are visible to everyone.

walkthrough.title: Step 3: Deduce
walkthrough.text: After questioning, put your punch cards back in the support and analyze your answers. Use the bottom section of your note sheet to cross off eliminated numbers and write down what you've learned about each Verifier's criterion. For example, if Verifier C checks whether the three numbers are in ascending order, descending order, or no order, and you proposed 1-3-5 and got a checkmark, you know the criterion could be ascending order (since 1-3-5 is ascending). But you haven't eliminated the other possibilities yet. You need more data points to narrow it down to exactly one criterion per Verifier.

walkthrough.title: Step 4: End of Round
walkthrough.text: Once all players have finished questioning and deducing, everyone simultaneously makes a fist and counts to 3. On 3, everyone points their thumb either up or down. Thumbs up means you think you've found the code. Thumbs down means you haven't figured it out yet. If nobody points up, start a new round. If one or more players point up, proceed to verification.

walkthrough.title: Verification and Winning
walkthrough.text: If you think you've cracked the code, write it clearly and secretly on your note sheet before the thumbs-up reveal. Once someone declares they've found it, each player who pointed up checks the Solutions section in the rulebook or uses the app to verify their code. If your code is correct, you win. If multiple players are correct, the one who asked the fewest total questions wins. Count every V and X on your note sheet. If it's still tied, all tied players share the victory. Prove your code is correct by composing it with the punch cards and showing it passes all Verifiers.

walkthrough.title: Incorrect Guesses
walkthrough.text: If you pointed thumbs up but your code is wrong, you are eliminated from the game. The remaining players continue playing. If only one player remains after eliminations, that player wins by default, even if they haven't found the code yet. So be careful. Don't guess until you're confident you've narrowed it down to a single possible code that satisfies every Verifier's criterion.

walkthrough.title: Key Reminders and Edge Cases
walkthrough.text: A few critical reminders. You must keep the same proposal for the entire round. You cannot change your punch cards between questions. You can question fewer than 3 Verifiers per round, and sometimes questioning fewer is strategically better. Your proposal and which Verifiers you question are public information, visible to all players. Only your answers and deductions are private. Put Verification cards back immediately after checking to avoid mixing them up. And remember: every Verifier is essential to finding the code. No Verifier gives redundant information. If you understand a Verifier's criterion, you don't need to question it again.

walkthrough.title: Solo and Cooperative Mode
walkthrough.text: You can also play Turing Machine solo or cooperatively. Go to turingmachine.info, select a problem, and try to solve it in the fewest rounds and questions possible using one note sheet. You can ask a maximum of 3 questions per round, just like the regular game. When you find the code, click 'Did you beat the MACHINE?' on the website to see how many rounds and questions the AI took. To win, you need to match or beat the machine's performance. In cooperative mode, simply work together with other players sharing one note sheet.

walkthrough.title: Handicap System
walkthrough.text: If an experienced player is facing newer players, use the handicap system. In the first round only, the experienced player fills in one or two answer squares on their note sheet before the round starts. These count as questions already asked, reducing how many questions they can ask in round one. Fill in 1 square for a slight skill difference, or 2 squares for a larger gap. For example, with 2 squares filled, the experienced player can only ask 1 question in round one instead of 3.

walkthrough.title: Welcome and Meet the Players
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through a scripted first round of Turing Machine with three players: Alex, Blake, and Casey. We'll use a 4-Verifier problem to keep things manageable. Each Verifier (A through D) has a Criteria card showing several possible criteria, and one hidden correct criterion. Our players need to figure out all four criteria and find the one 3-digit code that satisfies them all. Follow along even if you have more or fewer players at your table.

walkthrough.title: The Problem Setup
walkthrough.text: Here's our practice problem. Verifier A has Criteria card 4, which checks the yellow number compared to 4 — it's either less than 4, equal to 4, or greater than 4. Verifier B has Criteria card 9, which checks how many 1s are in the code. Verifier C has Criteria card 11, which compares the blue number to the yellow number. Verifier D has Criteria card 14, which checks which color's number is the smallest. Everyone, take a moment to read your Criteria cards and understand what each Verifier could be checking.

walkthrough.title: Round 1: Composing Proposals
walkthrough.text: All three players compose their proposals simultaneously. Alex pulls blue 3, yellow 2, purple 4, making the proposal 3-2-4. Blake goes with blue 1, yellow 3, purple 5, making 1-3-5. Casey picks blue 4, yellow 4, purple 2, making 4-4-2. Everyone writes their proposal on the first line of their note sheet. Remember, these proposals are visible to other players, but your answers will be private.

walkthrough.title: Alex Questions Verifier A
walkthrough.text: Alex decides to start with Verifier A, which checks the yellow number compared to 4. Alex's yellow number is 2. Alex slides the Verification card under the punch cards, aligns the corner symbols, and sees an X through the hole. That's a fail. Alex writes X under column A on the note sheet. Now Alex thinks: my yellow was 2 and I failed. If the criterion were 'yellow is less than 4,' I would have passed because 2 is less than 4. So the criterion is NOT 'less than 4.' It's either 'equal to 4' or 'greater than 4.' Good deduction.

walkthrough.title: Alex Questions Verifier C
walkthrough.text: Alex moves on to Verifier C, which compares the blue number to the yellow number. Alex's blue is 3 and yellow is 2, so blue is greater than yellow. Alex checks and gets a checkmark. Alex writes V under column C. So the criterion could be 'blue is greater than yellow,' which fits because 3 is greater than 2. But it could also be 'blue is not equal to yellow,' depending on the criteria listed on the card. Alex needs more information to narrow it down. Alex decides to save the third question for later. Smart move — sometimes two questions in a round is enough.

walkthrough.title: Blake Questions Verifier B
walkthrough.text: Blake tackles Verifier B, which checks how many 1s are in the code. Blake's proposal is 1-3-5, which contains exactly one 1. Blake slides the Verification card under and gets a checkmark. Blake writes V under column B. Blake now knows that having exactly one 1 passes B's test. But the criterion could be 'exactly one 1,' 'at least one 1,' or 'an odd number of 1s.' Blake needs another data point to be sure.

walkthrough.title: Blake Makes a Mistake: Not Reading the Criteria Card
walkthrough.text: Blake then questions Verifier D, which checks which color's number is the smallest. Blake's proposal is 1-3-5. Blue is 1, the smallest. Blake gets a checkmark. Excited, Blake immediately assumes the criterion is 'blue is the smallest.' But wait — Blake didn't carefully read the Criteria card. Card 14 actually lists three options: 'blue is smaller than yellow and purple,' 'yellow is smaller than blue and purple,' or 'purple is smaller than yellow and blue.' Getting a checkmark with blue as 1 is consistent with 'blue is smallest,' but Blake hasn't actually eliminated the other possibilities yet. One data point isn't enough.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Read the Full Criteria Card
walkthrough.text: This is a critical mistake new players make. Before you start questioning a Verifier, read ALL the possible criteria listed at the bottom of the Criteria card. A single checkmark or X only helps you eliminate possibilities if you know what all the possibilities are. Blake jumped to a conclusion without considering the full list. In Turing Machine, patience and thoroughness beat speed. Write down every possible criterion for each Verifier at the start, then systematically cross them off.

walkthrough.title: Casey Questions Verifier A
walkthrough.text: Casey also questions Verifier A. Casey's yellow number is 4. Casey checks and gets a checkmark. Casey writes V under column A. Now Casey thinks: my yellow was 4 and I passed. The criterion could be 'equal to 4' or 'greater than or equal to 4.' But remember, Alex got an X with yellow 2. If Casey knows Alex's proposal (which is public), Casey can combine that information. Yellow 2 failed, yellow 4 passed. That eliminates 'less than 4' and is consistent with either 'equal to 4' or 'greater than 4.' Very useful.

walkthrough.title: Casey Makes a Mistake: Confusing Criterion with Code
walkthrough.text: Casey questions Verifier C next. Casey's blue is 4 and yellow is 4, so they're equal. Casey gets an X, meaning the proposal failed C's test. Casey writes X under column C. Here's where Casey goes wrong: Casey mutters, 'The code must not have equal blue and yellow numbers.' No! The Verifier's answer tells you about the CRITERION, not the CODE. Getting an X means Casey's proposal doesn't meet the criterion. It means the criterion is not 'blue equals yellow' (since Casey's blue and yellow ARE equal and they got an X). The correct deduction is about what the criterion is, not what the secret code is.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Verifier Answers Are About Criteria, Not the Code
walkthrough.text: This is the single most common misconception in Turing Machine, and it trips up almost everyone on their first game. When a Verifier gives you a checkmark or X, they're telling you whether YOUR PROPOSAL passes THEIR TEST. They're not telling you anything about the actual secret code. The Verifier doesn't know the code. It only knows its own criterion. Your job is to figure out what criterion each Verifier is using, and THEN use all the criteria together to deduce the code. Two completely separate mental steps.

walkthrough.title: Casey Makes Another Mistake: Wasting a Question
walkthrough.text: Casey decides to use the third question on Verifier A again, even though Casey already got a checkmark from A this round. Casey's proposal hasn't changed (you can't change it mid-round), so questioning the same Verifier with the same proposal will give the same answer. Casey slides the Verification card under and of course gets another checkmark. That was a completely wasted question. Casey now has 3 questions recorded for round 1, while Alex only has 2. In a tiebreaker situation, that wasted question could cost Casey the game.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Never Re-Question with the Same Proposal
walkthrough.text: Since your proposal stays the same for the entire round, questioning the same Verifier twice in one round will always give you the same answer. It's a pure waste of a question. If you've already gotten useful information from 2 Verifiers, save your third question. Every question adds to your total count, and the player with the fewest questions wins ties. Be strategic about when to ask and when to wait.

walkthrough.title: Deduction Phase
walkthrough.text: Now all three players put their punch cards back and analyze their results. Alex has two useful data points and is already narrowing down Verifiers A and C. Blake has two data points but jumped to a premature conclusion about Verifier D. Casey has technically three questions recorded but one was wasted, and also confused criterion deduction with code deduction. The key during this phase is to use the bottom section of your note sheet: cross off eliminated numbers and write which criteria you've ruled out for each Verifier.

walkthrough.title: End of Round
walkthrough.text: Everyone makes a fist and counts to 3. All three players point thumbs down — nobody has enough information yet to confidently declare the code. Good. In a 4-Verifier problem, it usually takes 2 to 4 rounds to crack the code. Guessing too early risks elimination. A new round begins, and this time everyone can compose a brand new proposal to test different numbers against different Verifiers.

walkthrough.title: Planning Round 2 Proposals
walkthrough.text: Here's where smart play really shines. Alex thinks about what proposal would be most informative for round 2. Alex already knows the yellow number compared to 4 is either 'equal to' or 'greater than.' If Alex proposes yellow 5 next round and questions Verifier A, a checkmark would be consistent with both, but an X would eliminate 'greater than 4' (since 5 is greater than 4 and failed). So yellow 5 is a great test number. This kind of targeted proposal design is the heart of Turing Machine strategy.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You've now seen the full flow of a round: composing proposals, questioning Verifiers, reading answers through punch card holes, deducing criteria, and the thumbs-up reveal. You've also seen the three most common mistakes new players make: not reading the full Criteria card before questioning, confusing criterion deduction with code deduction, and wasting questions by re-questioning the same Verifier in the same round. Avoid those traps and you'll be cracking codes in no time.

walkthrough.title: Before You Crack the Code
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics on how to craft efficient proposals and deduce systematically.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every Criteria card type and what it checks.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the mistakes that trip up almost every new player. First, confusing what the Verifier tells you. Remember: a checkmark or X tells you about the criterion, not the code. Second, guessing the code too early. One wrong guess and you're eliminated. Third, wasting questions by re-questioning a Verifier you've already identified. Fourth, not reading all the possible criteria on the Criteria card before questioning. Fifth, forgetting to record your answers. If you avoid just these five mistakes, you'll already play better than most beginners.

walkthrough.title: Craft Targeted Proposals
walkthrough.text: The best players don't propose random numbers. They design proposals specifically to test a hypothesis. For example, if you need to know whether Verifier A's criterion is 'yellow less than 4' or 'yellow equal to 4,' propose a number where these two criteria give different answers. Yellow 3 would pass 'less than 4' but fail 'equal to 4,' so that's a perfect test number. Before each round, look at your remaining uncertainties and design a proposal that resolves at least one of them definitively.

walkthrough.title: Use Other Players' Public Information
walkthrough.text: Proposals and which Verifiers each player questions are public information. Use this. If another player proposes 1-5-3 and questions Verifier B, you don't know their answer, but you know what they tested. If you later figure out Verifier B's criterion, you can retroactively deduce what answer they got. More importantly, seeing what others are testing helps you avoid redundant proposals. Don't waste a round testing the same thing someone else just tested. Vary your proposals to cover different ground.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Verifiers with Fewer Criteria
walkthrough.text: Some Criteria cards list only two possible criteria, while others list three. A Verifier with only two possibilities can be identified in a single well-chosen question. Start with those. Once you've locked down the easy Verifiers, you can focus your remaining questions on the trickier ones. This is much more efficient than randomly questioning whatever Verifier catches your eye.

walkthrough.title: Take Good Notes
walkthrough.text: Your note sheet is your most powerful tool. Here's a system that works well. At the top of each Verifier column, write the possible criteria from the Criteria card before the game starts. Each round, record your proposal and every answer. In the elimination grid, cross off numbers you've ruled out. When you identify a criterion, write it in the dedicated space at the bottom. The players who win consistently are the ones with the cleanest, most organized notes. Sloppy notes lead to missed deductions and wasted questions.

walkthrough.title: Know When NOT to Question
walkthrough.text: You can question up to 3 Verifiers per round, but you don't have to use all 3. If you've already identified a Verifier's criterion, don't question it again. If your proposal wouldn't give you useful information from a particular Verifier, skip it. Every question you ask adds to your tiebreaker total. The winning mindset is: 'What is the minimum number of questions I need to be certain?' Not 'How many questions can I cram in?'

walkthrough.title: Cross-Reference Between Verifiers
walkthrough.text: As you narrow down each Verifier's criterion, the criteria themselves help you narrow down the code. Here's the key insight: every Verifier is essential, and no two Verifiers give redundant information. So if Verifier A tells you the yellow number is equal to 4, and Verifier C tells you blue is greater than yellow, then blue must be 5. Each identified criterion constrains the code further. Once you've identified all criteria, there should be exactly one code that satisfies them all. If you find two possible codes, you've made a deduction error somewhere.

walkthrough.title: Don't Guess Until You're Certain
walkthrough.text: This bears repeating because it's so important. Never point thumbs up unless you have identified every Verifier's criterion and confirmed that exactly one code satisfies all of them. If you think it might be code 3-4-2 but you're not 100 percent sure, keep playing. A wrong guess eliminates you from the game. The only exception is if you're in a desperate race and another player is clearly about to guess — but even then, an extra round of confirmation is usually worth it. The penalty for being wrong is far worse than the cost of one more round.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't confuse Verifier answers (about criteria) with code information
Don't guess too early — wrong guess = elimination
Don't re-question a Verifier you've already identified
Read ALL possible criteria on the card before questioning
Always record every answer on your note sheet

summary.title: Craft Targeted Proposals
bullets: Design proposals to test specific hypotheses
Choose numbers that distinguish between remaining criteria
Before each round, identify your top uncertainty and target it
One well-designed proposal beats three random ones

summary.title: Use Other Players' Public Information
bullets: Proposals and which Verifiers are questioned are visible to all
Avoid testing what another player just tested
Track others' proposals to retroactively deduce their results
Vary your proposals to cover different ground

summary.title: Prioritize Verifiers with Fewer Criteria
bullets: 2-option Verifiers can be solved in 1 well-chosen question
Start with easy Verifiers, then focus on harder ones
Identified Verifiers reduce the search space for all other criteria

summary.title: Take Good Notes
bullets: Write all possible criteria per Verifier BEFORE the game starts
Record every proposal and answer systematically
Use the elimination grid to cross off ruled-out numbers
Clean notes = fewer errors = fewer wasted questions

summary.title: Know When NOT to Question
bullets: Skip Verifiers you've already identified
Skip if your proposal wouldn't give useful info from that Verifier
Fewer total questions = wins tiebreakers
Goal: minimum questions needed for certainty, not maximum

summary.title: Cross-Reference Between Verifiers
bullets: Each identified criterion constrains the possible code
No Verifiers are redundant — every one narrows the solution
Once all criteria identified, exactly one code should satisfy all
If you find 2 possible codes, re-check your deductions

summary.title: Don't Guess Until You're Certain
bullets: Never guess unless you've identified ALL criteria
Confirm exactly one code satisfies every criterion
Wrong guess = eliminated from the game
An extra round is cheaper than a wrong guess

walkthrough.title: Information-Maximizing Proposals
walkthrough.text: The most powerful technique in Turing Machine is designing proposals that extract maximum information from every question. An ideal proposal resolves ambiguity for multiple Verifiers simultaneously. For example, if Verifier A is checking whether yellow is less than, equal to, or greater than 4, and Verifier C is checking whether blue is less than, equal to, or greater than yellow, then proposing blue 5, yellow 4, purple 3 tests both at once. Yellow 4 resolves A if you question it, and blue 5 vs yellow 4 resolves C's greater-than branch. Always look for proposals that let you question two or three Verifiers with one overlapping set of numbers.

walkthrough.title: Elimination by Contradiction
walkthrough.text: Instead of trying to confirm what a criterion IS, try to prove what it ISN'T. This is often faster. If a Verifier has three possible criteria, you only need to eliminate two to know the answer. Design proposals that would give a specific result if a particular criterion were true. If you get the opposite result, you've eliminated that possibility. For a 3-option Verifier, a single question can eliminate one or even two possibilities if you design the proposal carefully. Two well-placed questions can lock down a 3-option Verifier with certainty.

walkthrough.title: The Logical Lattice Technique
walkthrough.text: Expert players build a mental lattice of constraints. Each identified criterion adds a constraint: 'yellow must be 4 or 5,' 'blue must be greater than yellow,' 'there must be exactly one 1 in the code.' As constraints accumulate, the number of possible codes shrinks rapidly. After identifying two or three criteria, calculate all remaining possible codes and check which ones are still consistent. Often, a single additional constraint collapses the possibilities to just one code. This technique lets you solve problems faster by recognizing when you have enough information to deduce the code even without identifying every single criterion.

walkthrough.title: Early Game Strategy: Cast a Wide Net
walkthrough.text: In round 1, you know nothing. The best strategy is to cast a wide net. Choose a proposal with varied numbers across colors, like 1-3-5 or 2-4-1, so you test interesting cases for multiple Verifiers. Question the 2-option Verifiers first if possible — they're cheapest to resolve. Use all 3 questions in round 1 because you're unlikely to be wasteful when everything is new. The goal of round 1 is not to solve the problem. It's to eliminate as many criteria as possible and set up decisive proposals for round 2.

walkthrough.title: Mid Game Strategy: Targeted Testing
walkthrough.text: By round 2 or 3, you should have identified at least one Verifier's criterion and narrowed others to two possibilities. Now switch from wide-net to sniper mode. Design proposals specifically to resolve your remaining uncertainties. If you need to distinguish between 'blue less than 3' and 'blue equal to 3,' propose blue 3. If you get a checkmark, it could be 'equal to 3.' If you get an X, it's 'less than 3' (since 3 is not less than 3). Every proposal in the mid game should target a specific unresolved question.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Table: Competitive Intelligence
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to other players' behavior. If someone uses only 1 or 2 questions in a round, they may be close to solving. If someone suddenly starts questioning a Verifier they've ignored, they may have identified the others and are now closing in on the last one. When you see signs that an opponent is near the solution, you face a choice: spend one more round confirming to be safe, or guess now to beat them on question count. This read is crucial in competitive play. The best players balance speed against the elimination risk.

walkthrough.title: The Redundancy Principle
walkthrough.text: The rulebook tells you that every Verifier is essential — no Verifier gives redundant information. This is a major strategic clue. It means every Verifier's criterion eliminates at least one code that all the other criteria would allow. Use this during deduction. If you've identified three out of four criteria and they already narrow the code to, say, three possibilities, the fourth criterion must eliminate exactly two of those three. You can sometimes deduce the fourth criterion just by asking: which of its possible criteria would leave exactly one valid code? This meta-deduction can save entire rounds of questioning.

walkthrough.title: Extreme and Nightmare Modes
walkthrough.text: Once you've mastered Classic mode, the website offers two harder variants. In Extreme mode, each Verifier has two Criteria cards in front of it, but only one card contains the valid criterion. This doubles the search space per Verifier. The key strategy is the same — elimination — but you need to consider both cards simultaneously. In Nightmare mode, you don't even know which Verification card belongs to which Verifier. The cards are shuffled. You need to figure out both the criterion AND which Verifier it belongs to. These modes reward the logical lattice technique heavily, as constraints from identified Verifiers help you untangle the ambiguity.

walkthrough.title: Question Economy in Competitive Play
walkthrough.text: In competitive games, winning comes down to question economy. The player who finds the correct code with the fewest total questions wins ties. This means every wasted question is potentially the difference between winning and losing. Develop these habits: never question a Verifier you've already identified, never re-test a Verifier with a proposal that gives you the same info as a previous test, and always ask yourself whether you even need to question a third Verifier this round. Some of the best competitive games are won by players who solve the code in 6 or 7 total questions across 3 rounds.

walkthrough.title: Solo Mode Mastery
walkthrough.text: Solo mode is where you truly test your deductive skill against the AI. The machine consistently solves problems in surprisingly few questions. To compete, apply every technique: information-maximizing proposals, elimination by contradiction, the logical lattice, and strict question economy. Track your question counts across games and try to improve. For 4-Verifier Classic problems, expert players regularly solve in 5 to 7 questions across 2 to 3 rounds. For 5 or 6 Verifier problems, aim for 8 to 10 questions. Beating the machine is the ultimate Turing Machine achievement.

summary.title: Information-Maximizing Proposals
bullets: Design proposals that resolve ambiguity for multiple Verifiers at once
Overlap test numbers that serve double duty across Verifiers
One efficient proposal can answer 2-3 questions simultaneously
Always look for multi-Verifier synergies in your proposal

summary.title: Elimination by Contradiction
bullets: Prove what a criterion ISN'T rather than confirming what it IS
For a 3-option Verifier, eliminating 2 options identifies the answer
Design proposals that produce opposite results for different criteria
Two well-placed questions can solve a 3-option Verifier

summary.title: The Logical Lattice Technique
bullets: Build a mental web of constraints from identified criteria
After 2-3 criteria, calculate all remaining valid codes
Additional constraints rapidly collapse possibilities to one
Can sometimes deduce the code without identifying every criterion

summary.title: Early Game Strategy: Cast a Wide Net
bullets: Round 1: varied proposals (e.g., 1-3-5 or 2-4-1) to test broadly
Target 2-option Verifiers first — cheapest to resolve
Use all 3 questions in round 1 for maximum elimination
Goal: eliminate criteria and set up decisive round 2 proposals

summary.title: Mid Game Strategy: Targeted Testing
bullets: Rounds 2-3: switch from wide-net to sniper mode
Design proposals to resolve one specific remaining uncertainty
Pick numbers that distinguish between the 2 remaining possibilities
Each mid-game proposal should target a specific unresolved question

summary.title: Reading the Table: Competitive Intelligence
bullets: Watch opponents' behavior — fewer questions = closer to solving
New Verifier targeting may indicate others are already identified
Balance speed (guess now) vs safety (one more round to confirm)
Question count awareness is crucial for competitive play

summary.title: The Redundancy Principle
bullets: Every Verifier is essential — no redundant information exists
Each criterion eliminates at least one code the others allow
Use meta-deduction: which criterion leaves exactly one valid code?
Can sometimes identify a criterion without even questioning its Verifier

summary.title: Extreme and Nightmare Modes
bullets: Extreme: 2 Criteria cards per Verifier, only 1 valid criterion among them
Nightmare: Verification cards are shuffled across Verifiers
Same elimination strategy, but doubled search space
Logical lattice technique is essential for these harder modes

summary.title: Question Economy in Competitive Play
bullets: Fewest total questions wins tiebreakers
Never re-question an identified Verifier or redundant proposal
Always ask: do I even need a third question this round?
Top players solve in 6-7 questions across 3 rounds

summary.title: Solo Mode Mastery
bullets: Solo mode pits you against the AI's question count
4-Verifier Classic: target 5-7 questions in 2-3 rounds
5-6 Verifier problems: target 8-10 questions
Apply all techniques: max-info proposals, elimination, lattice, economy

entries.definition: A 3-digit code composed by overlaying three punch cards: one blue, one yellow, one purple. Each digit ranges from 1 to 5. The proposal stays the same for the entire round and is visible to all players.

A fictional entity (a form of artificial intelligence) that checks your proposal against one specific criterion. Each Verifier is identified by a letter (A through F). A game uses 4 to 6 Verifiers depending on the problem's difficulty.

The single specific rule a Verifier checks proposals against, such as 'the blue number is greater than 3' or 'the three numbers are in ascending order.' Each Verifier checks exactly one criterion, and your goal is to deduce which one.

A card placed in front of each Verifier that shows what the Verifier is checking and lists all possible criteria. There are 48 Criteria cards in the box. The correct criterion is one of those listed at the bottom of the card.

A card placed face down next to each Verifier's Criteria card. When slid under overlapping punch cards, it reveals the Verifier's answer (checkmark or X) through the punch card hole. There are 95 Verification cards in the box. Each can be used for 4 different numbers and 4 different colors.

A card with holes punched in it, used to compose proposals. There are 45 punch cards in three color sets of 15 (blue, yellow, purple), each numbered 1 through 5 with three copies per number. Overlaying three cards creates one hole through which you read the Verifier's answer.
The plastic stand that holds all 45 punch cards upright during the game. Assembled once and stored assembled in the box. Holds blue cards in front, yellow in middle, purple in back.
The central tile placed in the middle of the table during setup. Serves as the game's focal point around which Verifiers and components are arranged.
A card given to each player that doubles as a privacy screen to hide note sheets and deductions from other players. Included for up to 4 players.

A paper sheet (50 in the box) used to track proposals, Verifier answers (V or X), eliminated numbers, and deduced criteria. Has sections for each round, elimination grids, and space for criterion notes.

The positive answer from a Verifier, visible through the punch card hole. Means your proposal passes the Verifier's test — your proposed numbers satisfy their criterion. Recorded as V on the note sheet.

The negative answer from a Verifier, visible through the punch card hole. Means your proposal fails the Verifier's test — your proposed numbers do NOT satisfy their criterion. Recorded as X on the note sheet.

A specific puzzle to solve, defining which Criteria cards and Verification cards to use for each Verifier. The rulebook contains 20 problems (1-4 introductory, 5-20 standard). Millions more are available at turingmachine.info.
The first digit of the 3-digit code, represented by blue punch cards. Ranges from 1 to 5.
The second digit of the 3-digit code, represented by yellow punch cards. Ranges from 1 to 5.
The third digit of the 3-digit code, represented by purple punch cards. Ranges from 1 to 5.

entries.term: Card 1: Single Number vs Value (2 options)
entries.definition: The Verifier checks whether a specific color's number is equal to or greater than 1. Two possible criteria. Watch out: getting a checkmark does NOT mean the number IS that value, only that it meets the comparison.

entries.term: Cards 2-4: Single Number vs Value (3 options)
entries.definition: The Verifier compares a specific color's number to a fixed value. Three possible criteria: less than, equal to, or greater than the indicated number. For example, Card 4 checks yellow compared to 4.
The Verifier checks whether a specific color's number is even (2 or 4) or odd (1, 3, or 5). Two possible criteria per card.

entries.term: Cards 8-10: Count of a Specific Number
entries.definition: The Verifier checks that there is a precise number of 1s (or another specific digit) in your proposal. For example, exactly zero, one, two, or three 1s. The Verifier knows the exact count.

The Verifier compares two specific numbers within your proposal (e.g., blue vs yellow). Three possible criteria: first is less than, equal to, or greater than the second. Getting a checkmark with equal numbers does NOT mean the code's numbers are equal.
The Verifier checks which color's number is smaller than all the others. Three possible criteria, one for each color. The Verifier knows which color is smallest.

entries.term: Card 16: More Even or Odd Numbers
entries.definition: The Verifier checks whether there are more even numbers (e.g., 4-5-4) or more odd numbers (e.g., 3-4-1) in the entire code. Two possible criteria.
The Verifier checks for a precise count of even numbers in the code: zero, one, two, or three. The Verifier knows the exact count.
The Verifier checks whether the sum of all three numbers in the code is even or odd. Two possible criteria.

entries.term: Cards 19: Sum of Two Numbers vs 6
entries.definition: The Verifier compares the sum of the blue and yellow numbers to 6. Three possible criteria: less than, equal to, or greater than 6.

The Verifier checks if any number repeats in the code: no repetition (e.g., 1-2-5), one number repeats once (e.g., 1-2-1), or a number repeats twice (e.g., 2-2-2). The Verifier does not know which number or color repeats.

The Verifier checks whether there is exactly one pair of identical numbers (e.g., 3-1-3) or no pairs (e.g., 2-3-1, or 3-3-3 which is not a pair). If there is a pair, the Verifier doesn't know the color or value.

entries.term: Card 22: Ascending, Descending, or No Order
entries.definition: The Verifier checks whether the three numbers are in strictly ascending order, strictly descending order, or neither. Note: 2-2-3 is NOT ascending because all three numbers must ascend.

entries.term: Card 23: Sum of All Numbers vs 6
entries.definition: The Verifier compares the sum of all three numbers in the code to 6. Three possible criteria: less than, equal to, or greater than 6. Works like Card 19 but uses all three numbers.

The Verifier checks for consecutive increasing values: a 2-digit sequence (e.g., 3-1-2 has 1-2), a 3-digit sequence (e.g., 3-4-5), or none. Numbers must be consecutive (1-3 is increasing but not consecutive).

Like Card 24 but also counts decreasing sequences. Checks for a 2-digit consecutive sequence (increasing or decreasing), a 3-digit consecutive sequence, or none. The Verifier doesn't know if it's increasing or decreasing.
The Verifier checks whether a specific color's number is less than 3. The Verifier knows which color. Other colors' numbers can also be less than 3 — the Verifier just isn't checking those.
The Verifier checks whether a specific color's number is exactly 1. The Verifier knows which color. Other colors can also be 1 — the Verifier isn't checking those.
The Verifier checks whether a specific color's number is greater than 1. The Verifier knows which color. Other colors can also be greater than 1.
The Verifier checks whether a specific color's number is even or odd. Similar to Cards 5-7 but may appear on a different color.

entries.term: Cards 34-35: Color Less Than or Equal To All
entries.definition: The Verifier checks whether a specific color's number is less than or equal to all other numbers. No other color can be less than this one.

entries.term: Card 36: Sum is Multiple of 3, 4, or 5
entries.definition: The Verifier checks whether the sum of all three numbers is a multiple of 3, a multiple of 4, or a multiple of 5. Three possible criteria.

entries.term: Cards 37-38: Sum of Two Numbers Equals 4
entries.definition: The Verifier checks whether the sum of two specific color numbers (that they know) equals 4.
The Verifier checks whether a specific color's number is less than, equal to, or greater than 1. Three possible criteria.

entries.term: Card 42: Color Greater or Less Than Both Others
entries.definition: The Verifier checks whether a specific color's number is either less than or greater than both of the other colors' numbers. The Verifier knows which color.

entries.term: Cards 43-44: Color Compared to Specific Number
entries.definition: The Verifier checks whether a specific color's number is less than, equal to, or greater than another specific number that the Verifier knows.
The Verifier checks whether the number of 1s or the number of 3s in the code equals a particular count that the Verifier knows.
The Verifier checks whether a specific color's number is less than, equal to, or greater than another specific color's number. The Verifier knows both colors.
The standard game mode as described in the rulebook. Each Verifier has one Criteria card with one valid criterion. Available in the booklet and on turingmachine.info.
An advanced mode available on turingmachine.info. Each Verifier has TWO Criteria cards, but only one contains the valid criterion. Doubles the search space per Verifier.
The hardest mode available on turingmachine.info. Verification cards are shuffled so you don't know which Verifier each card belongs to. You must deduce both the criterion AND its assignment.
A single-player variant where you try to solve the problem in fewer rounds and questions than the AI at turingmachine.info. Maximum 3 questions per round. Uses one note sheet.
A team variant where all players share one note sheet and work together to beat the machine's question count. Same rules as solo mode but with group discussion.

A balancing mechanism for games between experienced and newer players. The experienced player fills in 1-2 answer squares on their note sheet before round 1, reducing their available questions. Only applies to the first round.

A situation that can arise with more difficult Criteria cards where players believe the machine has made a mistake. Occurs when the criterion interacts unexpectedly with the proposal. Explained in detail on turingmachine.info.

Each problem in the booklet has a difficulty rating. Problems 1-4 are introductory. Higher-numbered problems involve more Verifiers (5 or 6) and more complex Criteria cards, requiring more rounds and more sophisticated deduction.

The total number of Verifier questions a player asks across all rounds. Each V or X recorded on the note sheet counts as one question. Used as the tiebreaker when multiple players find the correct code in the same round.

If a player declares they've found the code (thumbs up) but their answer is wrong, they are eliminated and cannot continue playing. Remaining players continue until someone finds the code or one player remains.
